headline: The All Whites' Unbeaten Record Faces Its Sternest Test Yet
subheadline: New Zealand's fairy-tale tournament history collides with a Belgium side that just demolished the USA 5-2 and has De Bruyne fit and firing
openingNarrative:
Here is a statistic that will make you do a double take: New Zealand are unbeaten in tournament history. Three matches at the 2010 tournament produced three draws — against Slovakia, Italy, and Paraguay — and the All Whites went home with their heads held impossibly high, the only unbeaten team at that entire tournament. It is one of football's most charming records, and it walks into this match against a Belgium side that just put five past the United States.

Belgium arrive with genuine momentum. Kevin De Bruyne — whose fitness was a major doubt — has been declared fit and proved it emphatically in the 5-2 demolition of the USA. That performance was a statement: five goals, fluid movement, De Bruyne conducting from the center of everything. Youri Tielemans captains with quiet authority. The system is clicking.

The caveats exist: Romelu Lukaku and Leandro Trossard are both doubtful with fitness concerns. But the 5-2 USA win showed that Belgium can devastate opponents even without their traditional attacking pillars. If anything, the forced tactical adaptation — more pace, more movement, less reliance on Lukaku's physical presence — may make Belgium more dangerous against deep-sitting opponents like New Zealand.

New Zealand's qualification was emphatic — 5 wins from 5 in OFC qualifying, 29 goals scored and just 1 conceded, with Chris Wood netting 9 times. The Nottingham Forest striker is the focal point of a team that knows exactly what it is. Belgium should win this match. But if New Zealand can channel the spirit of 2010, when they held the mighty Azzurri to a draw, this could be the group stage's most romantic story.

tacticalBreakdown.title: The Chess Match
tacticalBreakdown.narrative:
Belgium's 4-2-3-1, energized by De Bruyne's fitness, will look to dominate possession and create through his vision and Doku's dribbling. The 5-2 USA result showed Belgium at their most fluid — multiple passing combinations, quick transitions, and finishing from every angle. Against New Zealand's deep block, Belgium will push fullbacks high, stretch the pitch, and probe for openings.

New Zealand under Darren Bazeley will set up in a pragmatic 4-4-2 that compresses into a deep 4-4-1-1 without the ball. Chris Wood serves as the lone aerial outlet, while the midfield four will be asked to cover enormous amounts of ground. New Zealand's game plan is clear: stay compact, deny space between the lines, and make Belgium beat them with crosses into a crowded box.

The danger for Belgium is impatience. The 5-2 demolition of the USA may breed overconfidence. If New Zealand survive the first 30 minutes, the Belgian frustration could build, the passing becomes speculative, and suddenly Wood is winning headers in the opposition half. It is an old formula, but it works.

The Lukaku/Trossard doubts may force Belgium into a more mobile setup that is actually harder for deep-sitting teams to handle.
tacticalBreakdown.keyMatchup: Jeremy Doku vs New Zealand's right-back — Belgium's dribbling wizard against the All Whites' defensive resolve

dataInsights.title: By The Numbers
dataInsights.eloAnalysis: Belgium (ELO: 1748) vs New Zealand (ELO: 1505) — a 243-point gap, one of the largest in the group stage. Belgium's 5-2 demolition of the USA amplifies the perceived quality difference.
dataInsights.formAnalysis: Belgium's March was emphatic: beat USA 5-2, drew Mexico 1-1, De Bruyne fit and firing. Qualified unbeaten with 5 wins, 3 draws, 29 goals. New Zealand were perfect in OFC: 5 wins from 5, 29-1 goal record, Chris Wood scored 9.
dataInsights.squadValueComparison: Belgium ($771M) vs New Zealand ($76.8M) — a 10:1 ratio. The financial gap is a chasm. But New Zealand's unbeaten tournament record suggests they know something about performing above their weight class.
dataInsights.injuryImpact: De Bruyne FIT transforms Belgium's outlook. But Lukaku and Trossard doubtful means Belgium may deploy a more mobile attacking approach. New Zealand's squad is thin — Chris Wood is irreplaceable. Belgium's depth, even with doubts, is immeasurably deeper.
dataInsights.bettingPerspective: Belgium are overwhelming favorites, and the 5-2 USA result has shortened the odds further. New Zealand's draw market offers the only intrigue — the All Whites' entire tournament history is draws.
dataInsights.historicalPattern: These teams have never met. New Zealand have never lost at a tournament — an extraordinary record. Belgium's 5-2 March result suggests they have the attacking firepower to end it decisively.

playerSpotlights.name: Kevin De Bruyne
playerSpotlights.narrative: De Bruyne against a deep-sitting defense is one of football's most fascinating tactical problems. After the 5-2 dismantling of the USA, his confidence is at maximum. New Zealand will pack the area between their midfield and defense, daring Belgium to find a way through. De Bruyne's response will be surgical: diagonal balls, disguised through-passes, and set-piece deliveries that turn dead balls into live threats. His fitness — confirmed after weeks of doubt — means Belgium have their most dangerous weapon fully loaded.
playerSpotlights.statline: 6 qualifying goals; FIT and firing after a 5-2 USA demolition

playerSpotlights.name: Chris Wood
playerSpotlights.narrative: Everything New Zealand do goes through Chris Wood. The Nottingham Forest striker is not just their best player — he is their identity. His 9 goals in OFC qualifying were scored with clinical efficiency. At the tournament, against defenders who tower over Oceania's best, Wood's intelligence and timing in the box will be tested like never before. But this is a man who scores Premier League goals for a living. He belongs on this stage.
playerSpotlights.statline: 9 goals in 5 OFC qualifying matches; Nottingham Forest's Premier League striker

playerSpotlights.name: Youri Tielemans
playerSpotlights.narrative: Belgium's new captain provides the midfield stability that allows De Bruyne to roam freely. Against New Zealand's compact block, Tielemans' ability to recycle possession, find the switching pass, and maintain Belgium's tempo will be essential. He is the metronome that keeps the Belgian machine running smoothly.
playerSpotlights.statline: Belgium's captain, midfield anchor, provider of tempo and stability

playerSpotlights.name: Liberato Cacace
playerSpotlights.narrative: The Empoli left-back represents New Zealand's new breed — a player good enough to compete in Serie A. Cacace's attacking runs provide New Zealand's best chance of creating something from open play. His ability to overlap, deliver crosses, and combine with midfielders gives the All Whites a dimension that pure defensive solidity alone cannot provide.
playerSpotlights.statline: Regular Serie A starter with Empoli; New Zealand's most important outfield player after Wood

theVerdict.title: The Verdict
theVerdict.narrative:
The 5-2 demolition of the USA demonstrated Belgium's attacking ceiling, and a fit De Bruyne against New Zealand's defense represents a mismatch that the All Whites' spirit alone cannot overcome. De Bruyne will find the key, Doku will provide the chaos, and Belgium's depth of attacking options will overwhelm New Zealand's defensive structure.

The Lukaku/Trossard doubts may force a more mobile attacking approach that is actually harder for deep-sitting teams to handle. If Belgium deploy Openda's pace or a false nine system, New Zealand's defenders face unpredictable movement rather than the physical aerial battle they are better equipped to contest.

But New Zealand will defend heroically for stretches. They will frustrate, they will waste time intelligently, and they will remind the world why their unbeaten tournament record exists. The first goal may not come until the 35th minute. But once it arrives, Belgium's quality and confidence will produce a comfortable margin.

New Zealand's unbeaten tournament record ends — but they leave with dignity.

alternativeScenarios.reasoning: New Zealand produce a defensive masterclass that limits Belgium to a single goal. Wood wins every aerial duel, the goalkeeper has the game of his life, and Belgium's frustration produces a narrow win that feels like an escape.
The most romantic result in football. New Zealand's unbeaten tournament record survives against a side that just put five past the USA. A draw that would enter tournament folklore.

closingLine: New Zealand arrive with the most unlikely unbeaten record in tournament history. Belgium arrive having just demolished the USA 5-2 with a fit De Bruyne. Something has to give — and the smart money says it will not be Belgium's attacking quality.
